planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in early spring; in zone 9 plant February–March. Suruga is a Japanese astringent persimmon that must be fully soft (bletted) before eating, unlike non-astringent types like Fuyu. Trees are partially self-fertile but produce more heavily with cross-pollination from another Japanese persimmon variety. In zone 9 Suruga ripens October–November; the orange fruits color up dramatically and can be harvested for drying while still firm.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, deep, fertile soil; Suruga is a large-fruited astringent persimmon from Japan, producing brilliant deep-orange fruits of 8–14 ounces with smooth, thin skin and exceptionally rich, complex, honey-sweet flavor when fully bletted (softened to near-liquid jelly consistency). Astringent persimmons like Suruga contain high tannin levels when unripe that create an intensely mouth-puckering sensation; the astringency disappears completely when fruits are allowed to ripen and soften fully on or off the tree. Suruga is specifically valued in Japan for hoshigaki — traditional Japanese dried persimmon — where peeled fruits are hung on strings and massaged daily over 4–6 weeks to produce a sweet, concentrated dried fruit with a crystalline sugar bloom on the surface. In zone 9 Suruga trees are productive and long-lived, with spectacular fall foliage adding ornamental value. The large fruits are less susceptible to fruit fly damage than non-astringent types because they are harvested firm and ripened off the tree.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Japanese Persimmon Cultivars
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Diospyros kaki 'Suruga'
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Persimmon Production in Florida

planting.note: Direct sow in fall; in zone 9 sow September–November for harvest November–February. Red Choi can also be sown January–February for a spring crop. Successive sowings every 3 weeks provide continuous harvest. Cool temperatures intensify the red-purple color; heat causes plants to revert toward green and bolt quickly. Baby leaves are harvestable 25–30 days from sowing.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in fertile, consistently moist, well-drained soil; Red Choi is a red-leafed bok choy (pak choi) selection producing compact rosette plants with deep burgundy-red to purple-red leaves with contrasting white or pale pink midribs — visually striking in both the garden and on the plate. The flavor is identical to standard green bok choy — mild, slightly mustardy, very tender — but the anthocyanin pigments in the red leaves indicate additional antioxidant content. Red Choi performs best and shows the deepest color in cool weather; warm temperatures cause the plants to green up and bolt quickly. The compact size and partial-shade tolerance make it ideal for intercropping under taller crops, growing in containers, or filling shady corners of the fall garden where other vegetables would fail. Harvest the whole head at 45–55 days or cut outer leaves for continuous baby green harvest. Stir-fry briefly to preserve some color, or use raw in salads where the red color provides striking visual contrast.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Red Choi Bok Choy Variety Notes
University of Hawaii Extension — Asian Brassica Varieties for Hawaii
Rutgers University — Red-Leafed Asian Vegetables for the Home Garden

planting.note: Direct sow in fall; in zone 9 sow September–November for harvest November–February. Tah Tsai is exceptionally cold-hardy for an Asian brassica, tolerating temperatures below 20°F, and is the best choice for overwintering green harvests in zone 9 without protection. Successive sowings every 3 weeks extend the harvest window. Plants hugging the ground create a natural cold-air barrier that further protects against frost damage.

directions: Direct sow in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Tah Tsai (also spelled Tatsoi or Ta Tsai, Brassica narinosa or B. rapa var. rosularis) is an Asian brassica that forms a distinctive flat, ground-hugging rosette of dark green, spoon-shaped leaves radiating outward from the center — an almost architectural form entirely unlike the upright habit of bok choy or komatsuna. The very dark, glossy leaves have a mild flavor similar to spinach with a gentle mustardy note, making Tah Tsai one of the most versatile winter greens for raw use in salads, baby green mixes, braising, and stir-fries. The flat rosette form protects the growing center from frost and allows Tah Tsai to survive temperatures that would kill most other leafy greens — down to 10–15°F. In zone 9 it is one of the most reliably productive cool-season greens, thriving through the entire fall-winter-spring season from September sowing through April before bolting in summer heat. The flat habit allows it to grow in partial shade beneath other plants in the fall garden.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Tah Tsai Variety Notes
University of Hawaii Extension — Tatsoi Production Guide
Rutgers University — Asian Vegetables: Rosette Brassicas

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Ichang Papeda is among the most cold-hardy citrus species, tolerating temperatures to about 0–5°F when established — making it suitable for zone 7 and a nearly indestructible rootstock or ornamental in zone 9. It is primarily grown as an ornamental, rootstock, or specimen tree for its exceptional cold hardiness; the fruits are used for juice and flavoring but are not eating citrus. Ichang Papeda has contributed cold hardiness to many citrus breeding programs.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Ichang Papeda (Citrus ichangensis, Ichang lemon) is a wild citrus species from the mountains of central China near Yichang on the Yangtze River — the hardiest true citrus species, capable of surviving brief temperatures near 0°F and making it valuable for cold-climate citrus experimentation far beyond the normal range. The large, fragrant, lemon-yellow fruits are highly aromatic but extremely acidic and seedy, used for juice, fragrance, and culinary flavoring rather than fresh eating. The distinctive winged petioles on the leaves identify the plant and give it an interesting ornamental appearance with an almost tropical look. In zone 9 Ichang Papeda grows vigorously and produces abundant, highly aromatic fruit with a distinctive floral-citrus fragrance used in Chinese cooking and perfumery. Ichang Papeda is a parent of the Yuzu lemon hybrid and has contributed cold hardiness to many important citrus breeding programs; it thrives in zone 9 as an ornamental citrus tree with exceptional cold insurance.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Research Center — Citrus ichangensis
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Ichang Papeda and Cold-Hardy Citrus
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Papeda Group Citrus

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–November or February–April. Yellow Clivia requires a dry winter rest period of 6–8 weeks with reduced watering to trigger spring flowering — this is often the most critical management factor. In zone 9 the mild, drier winters naturally provide the needed rest period. Divide congested clumps every 4–5 years in early spring after flowering; divisions bloom within 1–2 years.

directions: Plant in deep to partial shade in well-drained, organically rich soil; Yellow Clivia is a rare and highly prized yellow-flowered form of Clivia miniata, producing umbels of 12–20 nodding, funnel-shaped, pale to deep golden-yellow flowers on sturdy stems in late winter to spring — a striking departure from the familiar orange of standard clivia and extremely valuable in shade gardens where yellow-flowered plants are scarce. The strap-like, dark green, leathery leaves form architectural clumps that are ornamental year-round and provide deep-shade ground cover where few other plants will grow. Yellow clivia forms were once extraordinarily rare and expensive (individual plants sold for thousands of dollars in South African and Japanese specialty markets) but have become more widely available through tissue culture and selected open-pollinated seed. In zone 9 and 10 clivia thrives under established tree canopies, on north-facing exposures, and in the deep shade of eaves and overhangs — precisely the difficult situations where gardeners have few flowering options. Avoid replanting once established; clivia blooms most freely when root-bound.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Clivia for Florida Shade Gardens
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Clivia miniata Cultivars
South African Clivia Society — Yellow and Pastel Clivia Breeding
